cards.back: The process of creating a function from a function template for a specific type
When the compiler automatically instantiates a template based on function call arguments

cards.text: The function created from a template is called a {{c1::function instance}} or {{c2::template function}}.

cards.question: How many function instances are created from this template?
cards.answer: Two function instances: max<int> and max<double>
cards.explanation: Calls 1 and 2 both use int, so they share the same max<int> instance. Call 3 uses double, creating a separate max<double> instance.

cards.front: When does the compiler instantiate a function template?
cards.back: When a function template is called (or when the address is taken), the compiler instantiates it for the specific type(s) being used.

cards.text: You can explicitly specify template arguments using angle brackets: {{c1::max<int>(1, 2)}}

cards.question: What happens when you call this?
cards.answer: It instantiates add<double> and converts both arguments to double, returning 3.0.
cards.explanation: Explicitly specifying <double> forces the compiler to instantiate the double version and convert the int arguments.

cards.front: Do function templates themselves take up any code space in the compiled program?
cards.back: No. Templates themselves don't generate code. Only the instantiated function instances take up code space.

cards.text: Template instantiation happens at {{c1::compile time}}, not runtime.

cards.concept: Why template instantiation can increase executable size
cards.explanation: Each unique type used with a template creates a separate function instance in the compiled code. Using a template with many different types can lead to code bloat.

cards.front: What is the difference between a function template and a template function?
cards.back: A function template is the template definition. A template function (or function instance) is the actual function created by instantiating the template for a specific type.

cards.question: Will this code work?
cards.answer: Yes. x will be 0, y will be 0.0.
cards.explanation: Explicitly specifying the template type allows the compiler to instantiate getValue<int> and getValue<double>. T{} creates a zero-initialized value of type T.
